Conversely, when price fall, buyers purchase more, and sellers supply less. This interaction between buyers and sellers determine equilibrium price, where quantity demanded equal quantity supplied. If market price above equilibrium, there's excess supply, and sellers lower their prices to attract buyers. If market price below equilibrium, there's excess demand, and buyers bid up price. Over time, actions of buyers and sellers push market toward equilibrium.

Government can sometime intervene in markets by setting price controls, such as price ceilings or price floors. Price ceiling be legal maximum on price at which good can be sold. If ceiling below equilibrium price, it cause shortage, because quantity demanded exceed quantity supplied. Price floor be legal minimum on price at which good can be sold. If floor above equilibrium price, it cause surplus, because quantity supplied exceed quantity demanded.

Markets be usually efficient at allocating resources, market failures occur but. Market failures can result from externalities, public goods, or imperfect competition.
